commentBody:
My five favorite original clues from last week
(in order of appearance):

1. Characterize?  (5)
2. Suit in a certain suite  (4)
3. Epic beginning  (4)(3)
4. Style with spotty coverage?  (11)
5. Part between the shoulders  (4)


SPELL
EXEC
PART ONE
POINTILLISM
LANE
Well, SOMEBODYSCRANKY (that would be me).

If this puzzle was on a difficult day you could add me to the “SOMEONESCRANKY” list… I HATE it when constructors use adjectives like 49D. Oh well, it was easy and the crosses filled it in.

Rating:
8/10

Yea I’m cranky about this too. Mondays are getting extremely difficult for the day. Maybe I’m just not good at crosswords but I’ve done every Monday and Tuesday for months now and this one is going to take me over twice as long as average and I’m still completely stuck on the right middle portion. It may be the first one in maybe 6 months I just give up on and don’t solve. Some obscure cheese crossing a cat clue? Pet cat? Lap cat? Fat cat?

Whatever the clue about lying about the reason for doing something I have most of that and still have no clue what it could be,

I used to have at least one a week I felt I could do enjoyably. Maybe it’s back to minis.

And we’re back to Monday. PHEW. 
Nobody should be cranky on a Monday.
ASMR was new to me.
Wish we had more straightforward entries today like we had yesterday. ; )

Fun Monday, good theme for a Monday with the PENCIL SHARPENER and other cranked devices.  Slightly harder than usual for a Monday but within normal range.

My only "Something's amiss" square was I had WHEW instead of PHEW and took a while to think of PRETEXT.

A coffee bean shaped like a spleen would be a very odd shaped coffee bean, or vice versa. 
Trust me, I am currently looking at a CT scan of a spleen.
5D is a bit of a stretch… that’s not a standard function of the spleen except during fetal development and in disease states, so doesn’t make sense for a Monday puzzle!
I will skirt the Tamale Trap to point out that while you might find GYRO in a cafe in the U.S., in a [Taverna] it would be GYROS.

Three Mondays in a row of exceeding my usual average.  A bit frustrating.  As for some of the stickier items, I've always thought that phew should be whew.  Salad spinner was fairly foreign, especially for a Monday.  Though that was not even close to the "tingly feeling" clue for ASMR.  A completely left field item for Monday.  At the end, the puzzle software had a glitch, not allowing reviewing the puzzle for more than a second at a time.

I liked it. I rarely solve in the evening, but I'm starting to suffer from FOMO when it comes to the comments, so... here I am.

I've recently started checking xwstats daily; right now it says this one was hard; I tend to keep the tab open and refresh occasionally to see how things change as more data comes in.

Such an appropriate puzzle for the weather here in Portland today!

I've just CRANKed down the a/c temperature. 
Outside it's in the mid 90s and it looks like it will be even worse tomorrow. 
So, yeah, SOMEBODY'S CRANKY and that somebody's me.
 
The clash between such heat against the backdrop of firs and pines defies the senses. The heavenly PNW summers of open windows are a thing of the past.
And yes, these are absolutely complaints. Didn't I just tell you SOMEBODY'S CRANKY, lol?

The good news is that it has nothing to do with the puzzle, which was a mild Monday, perfectly breezy, though I started out with rImS instead of LIDS. 
And Soul from Seoul stopped me in my tracks, thinking music before carmake. 

Thank you for the puzzle, Mr. 
Rollfing. 
It even made me laugh at my own foul mood!

Okay then. Never heard of the acronym ASMR, though I've definitely experienced it IRL.

Here is the beginning of what wiki has to say about ASMR.  Sorry, but I'm not deleting the footnote numbers.

autonomous sensory meridian response (ASMR)[2][3][4] is a tingling sensation and pleasant form of paresthesia,[5] that usually begins on the scalp and moves down the back of the neck and upper spine. ASMR has been compared with synesthesia[6][7] and may overlap with frisson.[8] ASMR is a subjective experience of "low-grade euphoria" characterized by "a combination of positive feelings and a distinct static-like tingling sensation on the skin". It is most commonly triggered by specific auditory and visual stimuli, and less commonly by intentional attention control. [9]


It's been used twice before in a puzzle, but were Saturdays several years ago. Not sure if third appearance should have been a Monday.
Or maybe this is a thing everybody but me has been talking about all this time.

Hi Sam,
Thanks for training us 
on the Pioneer Zephyr, 
where we 16A for 1D.

Well, it took me 26 hours (2 hours in app), but I finally solved the Sunday. Definitely the closest I came to losing my streak.

I went through every single clue at least four or five times, even brute forced a few squares. I was so excited when I found a mistake (CaLEF/aMAS instead of CCLEF/CMAS). But, alas, no Gold Star.

My least confident crossings were EDEMA/DEE, TWERE/EWER, HORSIE/BRIO, and GENA/ANNE/AIKIDA [SIC]. I thought maybe three names could have been GEri/ArNE, since AIKIDA or IIKIDA was Greek to me.

After dinner (Dubrovnik in New Rochelle, highly recommended), I asked my mom to run through the puzzle with me. I read her every answer I wasn't 100% certain on as we drove home. She was able to confirm a lot but didn't have any corrections. Her confirming EDEMA was a big help in narrowing things down. She didn't know who either GENA or ANNE were and never heard of AIKIDA either. But saying the word out loud made me realize that Asian martial arts usually end with -DO. I was just so certain that XENa: The Warrior Princess was the alien leader, I never gave that square a second look. Leader? As in a prefix. Silly me...

@Sam -- It is remarkably often that I either actually or in spirit pump my fist and say, "Good one!" at your pictures/captions.  Today your caption took me by surprise, and there I went again.  You consistently brighten up the whole Wordplay column and comments experience with your wit.  Thank you!

Nice puzzle! Aside from starting with LegS at 1D, It was good fun with a a clever theme!  Nothing to make anyone cranky about, but that's just my idealism peeking out. There's always something to make someone cranky. And that's my realism making an entrance. 

I especially liked 27D's cute clue for site of some cookies solving to JAR. And HOME is where the heart is always makes me smile! It's so true, though I love to travel, it's by far my favorite place to be!  Especially when my husband is also home! And when we have Hendricks gin on hand...   And yes, definitely in that order....   

I mean as long as he's home he could always go out and get me some if needed, so there's no reason to reverse the order. Har!!

“Somebody’s spleeny today! What happened? Someone put your coffee beans through the pencil sharpener? Has your white cell count been cranked up to 21? Did the sliders not slide correctly? Has a dance crew moved in upstairs?  Has your ASMR gotten mixed up with your karma?”

I noticed two clues that refer to boxing.

45A [Former boxer Ali who voiced herself in a "Scooby Doo" series] is LAILA who I know is Muhammad Ali's daughter.

Then there's 67A [Boxing or bowling] which solves to SPORT.

I can't imagine why anyone would think that knocking your opponent bloody is a SPORT or even sportsmanlike.

I can think of a better sport, related to 29D except with a different clue, i.e., [How to have fun with a needle] THREAD. The SPORT is, of course, sewing! Hie thee hither.

"Shall we go eat some salad?"
"Lettuce."

(Just tossing that out there.)

I suppose pencil sharpeners are still around—for old people like me.

A funny pencil story: I was at a sushi restaurant recently where they hand out little ordering pads and pencils so you can write down what you want. In the next booth, a boy of about five had been put in charge of the order, and he was taking the responsibility very seriously.

Someone decided they wanted one more piece of ahi tuna, but the boy had already written “1.” His eyes started to fill with tears.

His dad said, “Don’t worry—just erase the 1 and write a 2.”

The boy stared at him. “Huh? How do I do that?”

“With the other end of the pencil,” his dad said. “Look.”

The boy watched, completely mesmerized, as the mark disappeared. Then he brushed away the little rubber crumbs and whispered, “Whoa. Cool.”

I only wish I could have seen his face the first time he met a pencil sharpener.
My average Monday time has been going up recently and I love it.  The puzzles have been really good.

I'm almost always happy to learn a new term like ASMR, and this was no exception.

But I found its inclusion in a Monday puzzle to be jarring (relative to solving a Monday puzzle) and inappropriate.

I wasn’t impressed with the answer for “Tops of cups”.  Call me a nit picker but the clue suggests that the answer is part of the cup.  ‘Twould have been fairer, IMHO, to give the clue as “tops for cups”.  As was Sam, I was new to the word PANCECREWS.

PB JELLY TIME! 3:52 as a personal best time. 

W00t!!!

SLIDER? 

A very nice Monday puzzle. Some effort required but not so much as to irk. I’d think newcomers to the puzzle will be pleased to solve it.

Sam,

Tops of cups **are** lips. LIDS are tops **for** cups. 

Perhaps a little tricky for a Monday.
Fun puzzle.  I enjoyed it.
Should have waited till morning for this tricky Monday puzzle. Def needed caffeine to complete.

This was a nice palate cleanser after yesterday’s puzzle, which I only just finished. I learned from the comments that I wasn’t alone in my aggravation, but I wish I’d followed Andrzej’s path and just said no. There was no joy to be found there.

This was a different story. Easy, sure. But enjoyable with a cute theme. Although I must note that there’s no crank on my salad spinner. I just put my hand on the big knob and slap it silly. (This can be very useful for venting your frustrations, like those that come from wasting precious minutes of your life on a puzzle that seems almost designed to annoy you.)

Suddenly I’m feeling hungry for some salad.

I accidentally did this one before I solved Sunday’s puzzle 🤦🏼‍♀️ Luckily, I was still able to solve Sunday and keep the streak alive. Coming up on 5 years in the next three weeks. There are still some hand cranked PENCIL SHARPENERs in classrooms, although my students rarely used them. 
JACK IN THE BOX made me think of the movie Elf 😆 
Spending this week preparing for our 10 day roadtrip to visit my last 3 states in the US! Traveling through Oregon, Washington, Idaho, Montana, Wyoming, North Dakota and finally South Dakota. Really excited to finally get to experience Yellowstone.
Do cups really come with LIDS in the US? I've never seen a lidded cup in my life.

Way too hard for Monday.  Monday is supposed to be an easy puzzle.  This was maybe Wednesday difficulty.  Some very obscure clues.   Enough already.  This has happened before.   You don't have to show off so-called cleverness on Monday puzzles.
Really enjoyed solving this one. Low level of trivia which is always good.

Can a dog owner please enlighten me: when would you command your pooch to SHAKE? From my childhood memories of dog commands, it was much more likely to be “Don’t shake!!!🫣😜😭” as the sopping dog emerged from a river, canal, lake or — very occasionally — bath, and joyously unleashed its cargo of water 💦 …

I object against clue 5 Down: "White blood cell producer said to be shaped like a coffee bean". In a healthy adult, this organ is not the primary producer of white blood cells; the bone marrow holds that title. It is much more accurate to describe the sought organ as an immune system training camp, storage unit, and filtering station rather than a manufacturing factory.
Not sure if this was harder than normal for a Monday or my brain isn't up to speed yet.

Very enjoyable, and mostly straightforward but a few puzzlers - ASMR? and had no idea what hip hop performers were, not helped by rims instead of lids. I had halos instead of 'auras' and couldn't see how the car clue would fit in. (How can I keep forgetting it's AUTO not 'car'?)

By the way, re another post - why would a small child not know what an eraser (yes, I know now it's not a rubber) was? I still tutor children after school and they definitely use pencils and ... erasers.

Well, I must be pretty good, because I just got the "You've solved 6,500,000 Mondays in a row!" pop-up.

I don't know what my actual total is, because at one point I switched from a gift subscription to one in my own name. So the real total is somewhere above 6,500,000. If I live for 70 more years, I might set the record, although I won't be able to provide proof.

A bit of a workout for me. Felt more like a Tuesday puzzle.

*Lots* of entries starting with A today: ACAI, ATONE, AURAS, ADO, ALOHA, ASSN, AUTO, AKA, AHAB, ABLE, ADHD, ASMR, ABET, ANA, ALL. And eight entries ending in A. 

15 + 8 - 3 = 20. (Just checking my counting.)

Today, the theme answers each triggered a strong response from my past.

PENCIL SHARPENER reminded me of the potent smell of pencil shavings that would strike me when I used to empty one. As I’m thinking about it now, I can smell it as if it were right in front of me.

JACK IN THE BOX.  I was scared out of my wits the first time Mr. Jack popped up, and I never played with it again.  Even now I remember it whenever I hear "Pop Goes The Weasel".  

SALAD SPINNER.   When a visiting five-year-old saw ours and asked what it was, I showed him how it worked, and that did it.  He kept coming back to it, taking it apart, spinning his action figures, putting his ear to it and listening to its noise, wearing the inner basket as a hat – it reminded me today how creative the young mind is, and that I need to spend more time with youngsters.  

So, your puzzle, Eric, not only gave me a terrific “Hah!” when I uncovered CRANKY, but also got me thinking outside the box. Thank you!

Good fun.  ASMR was today's post-solve look up.  

Our constructor's reference to "crank to start" Model T's brought back the memory of using agricultural equipment that was started with a crank.  

I was taught from the beginning not to wrap the thumb around the crank in case the engine kicked back, which would tend to remove the thumb from the hand.

For those who are feeling splenetic about the spleen, you might wish to check out this Youtube video made by the Harvard Medical School Class of 2016 for its traditional second year show: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=aEi_4Cyx4Uw As for me I am always splenetic.

For 1A [Tops of cups], I filled in ACES, thinking of the suit of cups--equivalent to hearts--in the tarot deck. Then I remembered it was Monday. The revealer did not come to me at all. The salad spinner we use at work is the size and shape of a garbage can, with an electric motor in the lid, which plugs into a wall outlet. The pencil sharpener I use at home is a tiny plastic thing with a blade, which I twist around the point of the pencil; other than that, I've used electric ones. I have not played with a Jack-in-the-Box in years--do they make electric Jacks-in-the-Box, nowadays? Musical offering of the day--Schubert's "Der Leiermann," last song of the cycle *Winterreise," here sung by Dietrich Fischer-Dieskau. IYKYK. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9gmAdXxNdmU&list=RD9gmAdXxNdmU&start_radio=1
Some found this a bit hard for a Monday. I found The New Yorker's puzzle rather easy for a Monday. YMMV https://www.newyorker.com/puzzles-and-games-dept/crossword/2026/06/15

Well that was a splendidly fun romp to start the week! 

And Eric, the thought of you wandering around cranking the air as you built the puzzle was a splendid lagniappe. 😊

Wishing all a fine week ahead!

I liked today's puzzle.  It wasn't too easy, and the revealer was clever and amusing.

And, as a nice bit of non-pharmaceutical side effects, I find myself being nostalgic for the smell of PENCIL shavings.  

And a side note about PENCIL SHARPENERS:  they either work really, really well, producing an incredibly sharp point, or really, really badly, breaking off the lead before the pencil is sharp.  Often the same sharpener can be both.
How is that possible?

There's an article in today's Times about solvers and their streaks: https://www.nytimes.com/2026/06/15/crosswords/streaks-breaking-keeping.html?searchResultPosition=1 I'm guessing that several of those who are cited in this article are regulars in these Comments. Anyone care to identify themselves?
Good puzzle but never heard of ASMR.  Also I strongly disagree that the top of a cup is a lid. I solved the puzzle fairly easily tho

I always thought the A of P.T.A. stood for ASMR - because the parents and teachers get that warm, tingly feeling helping the kids. 

But what do I know - I’ve always thought the SPLEEN is shaped like a BEET.

This was probably the most enjoyable puzzle I have ever solved! Definitely number one in my books. As soon as I solved the revealer and looked at the themed clues, I had to laugh out loud. Funny, cute and overall enjoyable. A few times I had to guess on entries and ended up being wrong. Good thing when I do my puzzles in the paper that I use pencil! Great way to start my morning. I loved it loved it loved it!

Enjoyable Monday; slow start for me, but everything fell together from working the crosses. And thought the reveal was cute and clever. And my puzzle find today - a Tuesday from June 29, 2021 by Alan Arbesfeld. Theme answers in that one (with 'literally' at the end of each clue): THEJACKBOX THENIPBUD THEBEWAY THEUPAIR THEPIESKY THEBACKDAY Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=6/29/2021&h=17a .....
I love it, Eric!

Isn't anyone going to translate ASMR???
I know I'm not alone.
It's NOT IN TRICKY CLUES because why address the single tricky clue/answer pair in the whole puzzle if instead one can force folks to read the column and then have them come up empty?!

I haven't done the Spelling Bee yet (unusual for me) so I'm 
headed there for some FUN!
ALOHA.

The columnist’s comment about pencil sharpeners made me feel ancient! Yes! Manually operated! It gave rise to such clear & happy memories of the metal crank-operated sharpener in the utility room in my parent’s house - permanently screwed into place on a wood shelf. As a geeky kid I found no greater joy than sharpening a whole box of No. 2s when school started. 

Loved the puzzle. Perfect Monday. Breezy, amusing and while I got the theme almost immediately there was enough unknown fill to keep my interest.

So I am always amazed at the commonplace things that are different in different people’s experiences.
This is not meant to be a criticism, or condescension, just an observation.
I go to Starbucks or any fast food restaurant, I get a cup of any drink, and there are a whole pile of lids waiting for me to put on top. It’s such a routine, ordinary part of my life that I don’t think twice about.

And yet here I see a slew of commentators, from the US and elsewhere, for whom that term or usage is apparently completely alien. 

Just goes to show you how we need to be wary of interpreting other folks’ experiences in the context of our own. If there’s a swath of people who don’t connect with cups having lids, think about all the really important assumptions we make about others’ lives that are totally different than our own!
I was cranky til I did today's puzzle. Grazie Eric for the ASMR~!

This one was tough for a Monday, as I solved about 20% over my average time. Still, I admired the puzzle, especially some of the clever clues: 17A, “Device that helps you make a point in class” for PENCIL SHARPENER; 27A, “Toy likely to be found in a pop-up store?” for JACK-IN-THE-BOX; 8D, “ Soul from Seoul” for KIA.

I did know what ASMR was only because last year the White House posted a video of handcuffed immigrants being loaded onto a plane in which the only sound was the clinking of chains and they labeled it as “ASMR.” Since I didn’t then know what that meant I looked it up.

My mother - born in 1933 - was a spark, always dramatic. All her life, her “hand flourish” when telling a telephone-related story (to the tune of “So I got on the wires and called up Mary to share the news!”) involved holding her left hand to her ear, while rapidly cranking her right hand in a small circle. My siblings and I still use it in loving memory and for the laugh, and explain to *our* grandchildren about old magneto phones that we have only seen in pictures or museums.
Really enjoyed this puzzle, but after finishing I had to check my calendar to confirm that I hadn’t slept through to Tuesday.

I buried this as a (fourth) reply to a comment by @Andrzej, below, but, given the argument about "cups," it warrants a new heading.

In college, I took a course "Intro to Linguistics." In one class, the professor, Tom Linehan, asked us, by consensus, to define "glass," "cup," and "mug." After the arguments had settled down, he then produced a dozen or so different drinking vessels, of different sizes, shapes, and materials--from a disposable red Solo, to a beer stein, to a champagne flute, and categorize them. More arguments.
Fun fact:  EDAM is made backwards.

Really fun and easy game...until...

1A My too-fast answer was rImS... and it stayed there throughout the game until I didn't get the happy music. What? I read my filled in squares over from bottom to top, since I usually err towards the bottom in my rush to finish. That's when I realized the NW made no sense at all. LIDS, of course, solved that easily. So much for being a smarty pants. 

I'm sure you are all smarter than I am, so I will go hop in the shower and off to my shift at the ER, and I'll check in on comments during the slow times. If there are any. Solve on!

ASMR???!!!  I was sure one of my Across answers had to be wrong -- and yet I was sure that nothing was wrong.

So I figured that ASMR had to be some ghastly, newly invented textspeak initialism.  But it seems it's a modern medical condition instead.  Which, while it may be more intellectually worthy, is still complete gibberish to me.

Why do you find remains of mammoths in a JACK IN THE BOX?  I thought a JACK IN THE BOX was something that sprang out of a crackerjack box -- or am I misremembering my childhood?  At any rate, the clue says "sites" -- so doesn't JACK IN THE BOX have to be a plural?

And do you have to "crank" a SALAD SPINNER to make it work?  Don't they have buttons you can push?  I wouldn't know -- I'm a salad tosser rather than a salad spinner and have never spun a salad in my life.

And, finally, all the theme answers are inanimate objects rather than people.  Meaning that SOMEBODY'S CRANKY really doesn't work for me.

Despite all that, a colorful, fun-to-solve puzzle that provided me with more than usual Monday enjoyment.

A fun puzzle, if too difficult for a Monday. 

Some problems:

“Tops of cups” — LIDS. Hmm, not quite. A lid is something else entirely. Once I reviewed tricky clues (in this case, incorrect), that opened up the rest of the NW.

Jack in the Box was ungettable for me, which really hindered the middle. Still not sure I fully understand (or even care about the theme).

ASSN for “association” feels too specific to be a satisfying answer for this clue. 

I guess I appreciate that this Monday had some fight in it, but I’m left unsatisfied when the actual answers don’t feel in line with their clues. I can’t imagine a newcomer feeling that this puzzle was manageable but YMMV. Onto Tuesday.
Ironic to see PETCAT on the day we are taking our dear Socks on his final trip to the vet.
Yup, 1 across cost me some time when I had to find my mistake!
I was on track for a new best for Monday and it took me 6 mins to discover that I made a typo and wrote "SOMEBOBY" 🤦‍♂️

A little World Cup poem made from the words in today’s puzzle
a/ the heart is where the pass is brazil? holland? the moment stands close the moment where the d/ run lights up the world a/ all all all

It's amusing that a substantial number of comments today are about yesterday's puzzle. I'm about to add another. There was a fair bit of grumbling about 87D [Lunged (for)] which solved to MADE A TRY. I certainly thought it an awkward clue and answer. Very late in the day someone asked if anyone had discussed the fact that it was a rugby reference. I don't think anyone had, and someone else was kind enough to explain that a TRY is the rugby equivalent of a touchdown. link below. https://www.nytimes.com/shared/comment/4gq204?rsrc=cshare&smid=url-share

Of all the nits to pick, LIDS on cups? SRSLY? What parallel universe do you inhabit? When I go to the Wawa and get my ginger lime Diet Coke (they have the fancy machine) I pour it into a plastic cup, and put a plastic lid on top to keep from spilling it on the way to the Jeep. But then I have to ask for the plastic straw at checkout. How is that saving the planet? I've already used way more plastic just getting to this point. Now that's a legit nit, or even gripe. I understand why they banned the plastic bags at the grocery store, but the straws? At least they still have them. I've stopped going to McDonald's because they give you paper straws, which are terrible, especially if you're trying to drink a milkshake.

Yes, SOMEBODY'S CRANKY.

Once again the website is screwy in Chrome. Asks me to login, tries to get me to upgrade, offers option to continue without upgrade, and when I select that it kicks me off. Restart didn't fix it this time. App on phone seems fine. :/

Super fun puzzle!  Theme was cute; clues were fresh; and, yes, I got hung up on LIPS and PANCECREW.  A bit of a challenge for a Monday, which is fine by me.  Somebody might be cranky, but it's not me.  Thank you!

How about 

“Cups of Tops”: BRAS
15 Across was “speaker stands”, and the answer was “PODIA”. I’m a home audio enthusiast and have never heard speaker stands referred to as that. What am I missing?
The organ in 5 down isn’t where white blood cells are produced, it’s just where they reside after they are produced at other sites (bm, thy)
This was a little tricky and punny for a Monday, but quite enjoyable in the end.

I'm working in the archives and needed to enter a rebus. In past i've always used the ESC key to open the square for multiple letters (I solve in Chrome), but it seems they have changed something. Now when I hit ESC the cursor jumps to the settings icon near top left of screen. Anyone else noticed this?  It was fairly tedious having to move the cursor to click on the rebus button at top right every time I needed in--it was a puzzle with about 15 rebus squares. I haven't gone to settings 15 times in all my years of solving! :/

Either that was the hardest Monday I've ever solved, or my wits have dulled to the point where even a pencil sharpener would not avail me. 
Possibly both. 
P.S. I don't drink coffee. I think it's gross. Any clue requiring that I know the shape of a coffee bean discriminates against abstainers like me. I assure the puzzle composer, the editors, and the New York Times that my lawsuit is being filed even as we speak. The subpoenas may take a while to arrive, I'm having a very slow Monday...

Surprised to see this many comments on what to me was a very straightforward Monday puzzle, solved in under my average time. *shrug*

FWIW I'm Gen X, and used crank-operated pencil sharpeners in elementary school :-)
Ahhhh...now that I've read Sam's comments on today puzzle, I get it....sort of....to me the theme is a bit of a stretch! And FTR I love coffee!
Bowling is not. No defense, no strategy, no weather. It’s an activity.
wHEW! Finished it, even under false wRETEXT.
Maybe more of a Tuesday puzzle, but I mostly enjoyed it. I admittedly had never heard of speaker stands being pluralized in such a manner!
SO GOOD! I love the theme! Definitely didn’t leave me feeling cranky! Thank you!!

ASMR? Never heard that term and was convinced that must be where my solving erred. Apparently not. Is this an acronym some are familiar with? I consider myself pretty well read and fluent in English but this is a completely new one for me.

One more late puzzle find. One of the more amazing I've ever encountered - a Wednesday from August 2, 2023 by Barbara Lin. Four theme answers: TICTACTOETACTIC KAZOOBAZOOKA GOFARINFARGO TORMENTEDMENTOR Wow. Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=8/2/2023&h=46a ...
I admit it's clever, but it IS a Monday puzzle and now I'm wondering if I’ll feel stupid all week.🤔
The theme for today's puzzle is a hand crank

I also tried both "rims" and "lips" for 1A before finally figuring it out. My time on this was definitely slower than my Monday average, but I had fun solving it. (And it is perhaps extra funny that I struggled with "lids" on a day when I got a latte to go in a paper cup ... with a plastic lid on it.)
Relatively hard puzzle for a Monday, but fun nonetheless
Ms. De Armas seems to be in more crossword puzzles than movies these days
very fun puzzle! really enjoy- oh hello again ana de armas

My favorite encore clues from last week:

[It might have many sides]  (4)
[Ghost story?]  (5)


MENU
ATTIC

@RL

So I want to make sure that I am saying this in a way that is not condescending but helpful, please take it that way. When you do figure out the cheese, I suggest you memorize it, it’s the go-to crossword cheese and has been used 188 times in the NYT alone since 1994, that’s about once every two months. It won’t be the last time you see it.
And I find it a very tasty cheese.

@SP

And referred as "crossword cheese" on a famous sketch comedy TV show

@Barry Ancona

And not all that uncommon.

@RL

Like SP, I hope this doesn't come across as condescending or patronizing, but the feeling you express in the first paragraph sound awfully familiar.

I know exactly what you mean and I'd bet everyone here has gone through something very similar.  

So, my humble advice would be to dispense with thoughts like "...maybe I'm just not good at crosswords..." because it's purely a self-improvement project so there's no need for "good" or "not good".   Unless you want to officially compete, and in that case practically no one is very good.  There are solver here more than twice as fast as I am, and they don't consider themselves competitive (if I'm understanding them right).

Again, purely in my humble opinion, the best way to think of this spate of more difficult Mondays as a training regime.  One way or other you'll get better and better anyway (most of us do), but this extra resistance will be like running uphill--dispiriting, but ultimately beneficial.

But first, I know I always need to take some time to get over the frustration.  It's temporary.

@RL,
Along with the other comments, know that sometimes, you’ll be on the same wavelength as the constructor and other times nothing will “click”. That’s normal, and can lead to difficulties completing the puzzles, …or to personal best times! 

Also, a suggestion might be to actually write in your best guess - for example on the 49D clue, you mentioned a few possibilities. Which do you think is the best?  Actually put those letters in and see if it helps jog the brain on any across clues. 

Finally, walk away from the puzzle for an hour. You’d be surprised how many times you come back to it and fill in an answer you previously couldn’t “get”.

When I moved here in 2000, it got to 85° only 2 or 3 times for the whole of the summer, for a maximum of 2 or 3 days each time. A lot of homes didn't even have a/c. 

At night, we would open all the windows for what we called "nature's a/c" and shut them in the morning, along with the blinds and curtains while we went off to work. 
At most, we would run fans, or dip our feet in iced water if we were home during the day.  A bag of frozen peas on the forehead was yet another trick that did wonders. 

But it's gotten to a point where most people are getting window a/c units or mini split units installed. It's become otherwise quite dangerous, especially for the elderly.

@Vaer

With the personal anecdote door now wide open, here's mine:

60 years ago, long before the ASMR acronym was coined, a nice 8-year-old girl who sat next to me in the third grade had more paste in her paste jar than I had in mine, and shared some.  As she scraped the paste into my jar, I had the most wonderful, tingly feeling in the back of my neck and head.  I was mesmerized, and later was able to re-create it with another friend out on the playground where we slowly rubbed sticks on the back of each other's hands.

It was so memorable that, decades later, when a technology called web searching had been developed, I Googled it and found an article by or about the woman that first put the words autonomous sensory meridian response down on paper.  That led to countless examples of stories from people who had experienced something very similar to my paste jar episode. Later would come the videos that are commonplace today.

ASMR is a wonderful feeling when experienced, but very hard to conjure up on demand.  It still amazes me that my initiation experience was so profound that I would remember it so many years later, when other details of the same period are blurry and forgotten.

@Grumpy

My father grew up when the three most popular sports in the USA were baseball, boxing, and horse racing.  It was the era of Babe Ruth, Jack Dempsey, and Man O'War.  I still remember listening with him to radio broadcasts of the Friday Night Fights from Madison Square Garden.

Whereas in some of our major sports the players have outgrown the specifications of the playing fields, (think basketball and hockey) and others in which changes in technology/equipment have made them unrecognizable to yesterday's stars, (think golf and tennis), the weight classes in boxing ensure some degree of continuity and comparability between eras.  The heavyweight division is an obvious exception.

Nevertheless, there have been major advances in medicine and physiology that have advanced training, diet, and weight management that make it unlikely that Sugar Ray Robinson of 1950 could beat today's 147 lb champion.  But it's  possible to consider the question.

With the Marquess of Queensberry rules, boxing remains a "sweet science" compared to MMA.  Hobbes could have been speaking of MMA when he coined the phrase:
"Nasty, brutal, and short."

ASMR stands for autonomous sensory meridian response. (Wonder why they abbreviate it??). Some people find ASMR either relaxing or stimulating/tingling (like hairs on the back of the neck). Interestingly, it ties to the earlier answer, ADHD, in that ASMR can provide focus to some people with ADHD.

Hi, @The X-Phile!
I thought on you yesterday, and your comment from last Friday on ANEMONE. I was hiking in the Pigeon River State Forest, in Northern Michigan, and came across many anemones, bobbing in the wind, especially in the wet areas along the river. All the North American species I know are white, but apparently some around the Mediterranean are brilliant red, hence the connection with Adonis' blood.

It has always struck me how, in Greek mythology, young men (Adonis, Hyacinthos) are metamorphosed into ephemeral wildflowers, whereas young women (Daphne), become trees or shrubs.

Sorta like that episode from *Lost in Space*, with the Carrot-man.

@Bill

It's wonderful to hear that you remembered the myth while you were out hiking.  It never ceases to amaze me how these old stories continue to enrich our lives.

The idea of drops of blood undergoing "metamorphosis" reminds me of my favorite myth of all time, the birth of Aphrodite, as told by Hesiod in his "Theogony".

Cronos seized power by castrating his father Ouranos.  Cronos took the "severed member" of his father and threw it into the sea.  Where they landed the sea began to foam, and from this foam emerged Aphrodite (whose name can be translated as "born of the foam") fully grown.
Where each drop of blood from the genitals landed in the sea was born one of the Furies.
[Those were potent genitals!]

@The X-Phile The Theogony is a weird mish-mash of stuff, ain't it. Don't forget the drops which landed on earth (Gaia, natch)--those became the Meliae, ash-tree nymphs. Why specifically ash trees, and not oaks or beech? I don't know if you share my tastes in music, but here is one of my favorite pieces of orchestral writing, "The Birth of Venus," from *Trittico Bottecelliano* by Ottorino Respighi--you can almost hear the sunlight glittering on the water: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=08bm32R5UfM&list=RD08bm32R5UfM&start_radio=1

Some theme answers: SHEEPTRICK FISHANDSHIPS MUSICALSHARES SABRINATHETEENAGEWISH SHOPPINGBLOCK SHEIKTOSHEIK CASHPHRASE Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=9/17/2000&h=111a I'm done. ...

@Mean Old Lady Well, that's a rabbit hole I wish I hadn't dove into! I have heard of it but wasn't sure of the meaning. Wikipedia provides copious details here: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ASMR The short version - stands for autonomous sensory meridian response, a name given by Jennifer Allen in an online forum in 2010, to denote a tingling sensation and pleasant form of paresthesia, that usually begins on the scalp and moves down the back of the neck and upper spine. Interestingly, they also cite a Virginia Woolf passage from Mrs. Dalloway with a similar sensation described thusly: A nursemaid speaks to her male patient, "deeply, softly, like a mellow organ, but with a roughness in her voice like a grasshopper's, which rasped his spine deliciously and sent running up into his brain waves of sound". There's a lot more info in the article...

@SP
There are a billion Chinese who have a lid for their cup, to keep their tea hot. Funny how that never caught on everywhere else.

@Barry Ancona

Yes you are right, for many people it’s more a linguistic difference, that a lid is something apart from the cup that you put on top of it as opposed to a part of the cup that is the top. But even that contributes to my point, because it would never have occurred to me to consider a LID as anything other than the top of a cup (or for a cup, or on a cup, makes no difference to my brain). But clearly it makes a difference to other people’s brains, and it just reminds us that we are all wired differently and we need to respect that.

@D

Again this goes to my point I made earlier about lids, it’s amazing the stuff that is different about our experiences that we take for granted. A jack-in-the box was such a ubiquitous toy when I was growing up, and such a cultural icon (along with the song that went with it—“pop goes the weasel”)—that I can’t imagine anyone not knowing about it; even if you never had one, I would see it all the time in cartoons or movies or TV shows. Again, not being condescending, I respect the fact that you are an intelligent, worldly person who just never came across one or didn’t notice it if you did. As I said, it just amazes me, though, the gulf between people that crossword puzzles can evoke.

@Anon - I bumped on this, too. Then I remembered that some WBS mature in the white pulp of the spleen, so in that sense, the final steps of "producing" some WBCs does occur in the spleen, so it's probably OK for a crossword, which is not an academic treatise on immunology or cell biology.

@Asher B. Finally, a kindred spirit. I don't think coffee is gross. I love coffee. But I can't have it because the caffeine in it keeps me awake. I've also given up chocolate. And I am a total chocoholic. It breaks my heart. I'm sure that the many commenters here who say they can't get started on the puzzle in the morning without their cup of Joe realize that that is a sign of their addiction. Their brains are now programmed to get the extra stimulants the caffeine provides. If they skip the cup, they feel drowsy. They may even get a "withdrawal headache." A cup of the good stuff cures the headache--until tomorrow. The NYT article referred to today in the comments about people's relationships to the crossword "streaks" rings true with coffee. Coffee lovers can have the same liberating feeling if they give up their addiction as some puzzlers have when they forget about their streaks!! https://www.nytimes.com/2026/06/15/crosswords/streaks-breaking-keeping.html?searchResultPosition=1
